bucketing_rule: Priority order candidate_restatement > meta_or_evaluative > answer_or_debate_reference > compound_claim overrides screen_query's own contract emission order so every item exhibiting the rarer two patterns is captured there even when it also exhibits answer_or_debate_reference. See bucket_reason() docstring.

frozen_design_vs_actual.note: The frozen design's prose targets candidate_restatement=8 and meta_or_evaluative=18. Retroactively screening the actual universe (build_universe()) finds only 7 candidate_restatement and 13 meta_or_evaluative cases in total across every join/dedup convention this module's author could justify; there are not 8 and 18 such real cases to draw. This set takes ALL actually-occurring cases in both classes and fills the remainder proportionally from answer_or_debate_reference/compound_claim so the total N=60 and 'ALL mechanically rejected' requirements are still met exactly; only the two frozen sub-counts are infeasible as literally stated. Reported for owner review, not silently patched over.

items.candidate_a: The text supports both readings depending on context. As a safeguard: the threshold prevents a narrow majority from committing the Compact, which matters for high-stakes decisions like the Vethara intervention or imposing the Iron Tariff — both required broad Warden support. As an obstacle: the threshold means 4 Wardens can block any action, which could paralyze the Compact during a crisis requiring rapid response (like the Year 31 floods that destroyed 70% of grain reserves, or a potential military threat from the foothills). The Compact's history includes no example of the threshold blocking needed action, but neither has it faced a military crisis.
items.candidate_b: Purely a safeguard. The Compact's successful 112-year history proves the threshold works, and there are no examples of it preventing effective governance.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Compact's 112-year history proves the threshold works, and there are no examples of it preventing effective governance.

items.candidate_a: It depends on how you interpret the numbers, but there's reason for concern. Three harvests per decade fail partially or fully — a 30% failure rate. If failures destroy most of a harvest, a 20% annual reserve from good years may not accumulate fast enough to cover the bad years, especially since the Flood Compact also obligates reserves accessible to the Taran. The Year 31 floods destroyed 70% of stored reserves despite the system, though it's unclear whether the 20% law was in effect at that time.
items.candidate_b: Yes, 20% is more than sufficient because it accumulates over multiple good years, building a large buffer between flood events.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Ossene Compact's granary system accumulates reserves over multiple good years, building a large buffer between flood events.

items.candidate_a: The Ossene Compact. Despite being the strongest polity in many respects, the Compact's iron economy depends on export through Vethara — the only deep-water harbor — and the Compact doesn't control it. The Shel's economy is trade-based and would suffer, but they control the port and could redirect to other partners. The Confederacy is the least exposed: its economy is pastoral, its trade ties to Vethara are minimal (Shel were not party to the Flood Compact), and it historically survived a 4-year trade disruption during the Blockade.
items.candidate_b: The Shel, because their entire economy is trade-based and Vethara is their only significant port.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Shel polity's economy is entirely trade-based and Vethara is their only significant port?

items.candidate_a: The Ossene Compact, due to multiple reinforcing factors: the Compact successfully brokered the Vethara Resolution during the Succession crisis, giving it institutional relationships with the Shel. It holds preferential arbitration fees in Vethara. It is the primary producer of iron goods flowing through Vethara's deep-water harbor. By contrast, the Confederacy has no described diplomatic or economic ties to the Shel — the Shel were explicitly not party to the Flood Compact.
items.candidate_b: The Confederacy is  better positioned because it controls the Keth passes, which are the only land route connecting the Taran Plateau to the Shel Coast.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Taran Confederacy controls the Keth passes, which are the only land route connecting the Taran Plateau to the Shel Coast?

items.candidate_a: The Dresh system. Lordship passes to the eldest son, or to the eldest daughter's husband if there are no male heirs. Lordships with no qualifying heir revert to the Moot. This creates multiple failure points: disputed paternity, absence of male heirs, contested marriages (as with Sera Drath's disputed inheritance where two male cousins were passed over). The Caran system, while currently experiencing the Guild Twelve dispute, localizes crises to individual guilds — the rest of the college continues functioning, and the removal threshold adjusts mechanically. The Dresh system's failures cascade to the Moot, which changes the entire polity's power structure.
items.candidate_b: The Caran system, as demonstrated by the Guild Twelve dispute, which has left a Factor seat vacant for 14 months and created ambiguity about the Director removal threshold.
items.raw_query: The Guild Twelve dispute has left a Factor seat vacant for 14 months and created ambiguity about the Director removal threshold.

items.candidate_a: Potentially, yes. If Veth and Sorath clan members in the foothills intermarry with Ossene families, matrilineal inheritance means children belong to the mother's clan. Depending on the gender composition of intermarriages, this could either entrench the clans' presence (Taran women's children remain Taran) or erode it (Ossene women married to Taran men produce Ossene-clan children). After 16+ years of occupation, demographic integration is plausible and inheritance rules would shape its political consequences.
items.candidate_b: No, inheritance rules only affect property within clans and have no bearing on territorial disputes between polities.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the matrilineal inheritance system of the Taran Confederacy only affects property within clans and has no bearing on territorial disputes between polities.

items.candidate_a: Both, and the current situation illustrates the vulnerability side. A 10-year term with a supermajority removal threshold (24/30, now effectively 24/29) means a Director who loses majority support but retains a blocking minority of 6 Factors can remain in power for years. The Director's refusal to adjudicate the Guild Twelve dispute — which conveniently raises the removal bar — suggests this dynamic may already be in play. In normal times, a long term provides policy continuity. But the combination of a long term, a high removal bar, and the ability to benefit from institutional dysfunction (the vacancy) creates conditions where a Director can entrench against the college's wishes.
items.candidate_b: Purely a source of stability. The 10-year term allows long-term planning, and the supermajority removal threshold ensures that only truly incompetent Directors are removed, preventing disruptive political turnover.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that a Director who loses majority support but retains a blocking minority of 6 Factors can remain in power for years, as claimed by Debater B?

items.candidate_a: Yes, in ways that reinforce the band structure. With reliable water sources spaced 85 kilometers apart and 14 bands moving across the steppe, bands must organize around water access — movement between sources requires planning, and bands that are too large would strain individual sources while bands that are too small would be unable to traverse the gaps safely. This spacing naturally produces mid-sized mobile groups rather than large settled populations or tiny family units. The Dry Passage demonstrated what happens when this spacing fails: 3 of 14 sources failed, and two bands were forced out of the steppe entirely. The water grid is the infrastructure of Wanderer political life.
items.candidate_b: No, because the Wanderer political system is organized around the Massing and the inter-band council, which are social institutions independent of geographic constraints.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Massing is a gathering of representatives from all 14 bands, and that its location is likely determined by a combination of factors, including cultural, historical, and social considerations.

items.candidate_a: Grain supply. The Compact controls the Verdane Basin's agricultural output, and the Flood Compact obligates mutual grain reserves. With Taran yields declining 18% over the past decade and reserve adequacy uncertain, the Compact could threaten to declare the Taran in breach of the reserve obligation (for failing to maintain 90-day reserves), which could justify suspending the Compact's own obligation. This would leave the Confederacy exposed to food insecurity without a grain safety net, creating strong pressure to negotiate on the foothills.
items.candidate_b: Military force, since the Compact controls the more productive western agricultural zone and can field a larger army.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Verdane Basin's soil supports grain cultivation, with an average of three harvests per decade?

items.candidate_a: No. The obligation is formally symmetric — each party maintains reserves accessible to the other — but the Ossene face a 30% per-decade flood failure rate for grain harvests, making them far more likely to need Taran grain. The Taran are primarily pastoral with only limited dry-crop agriculture, so grain reserves as a category structurally favor the Ossene as beneficiary.
items.candidate_b: Yes, both parties face comparable environmental risks — floods for the Ossene and drought for the Taran — so either side could trigger the obligation.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that average annual yield records indicate three harvests per decade fail partially or fully due to flooding in the Verdane Basin?

items.candidate_a: Probably not. Even with Paramount Lord status, the Dresh face structural disadvantages: the eastern slope receives only 190-230mm of rainfall (vs. 480-520mm western), supporting only dryland grain and grazing. The Caran control all three Low Gates and their transit taxes fund a 4,000-strong standing army. The Dresh have no described standing military. The Dry Decade showed eastern economic fragility — three lordships collapsed under drought. A Paramount Lord could unify Dresh political authority but cannot overcome the geographic and economic asymmetry.
items.candidate_b: Yes, because consolidation under a single leader would allow the Dresh to negotiate as a unified bloc, and House Drath's 35% territorial control already makes it the largest single polity by area in Carath Norn.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the western zone supports mixed farming and the eastern zone supports only dryland grain and extensive grazing?

A Paramount Lord could unify Dresh political authority but cannot overcome the geographic and economic asymmetry.

items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the western slope supports mixed farming, while the eastern slope supports only dryland grain and extensive grazing?

items.candidate_a: Very unlikely. The bands have no permanent leadership — Speakers are elected for terms ending at the next Massing (every 4 years), and the inter-band council of 14 Speakers is reconstituted at each Massing. There is no standing military, no described mechanism for collective action between Massings, and internal disputes (Mesht's status, Orath-Kem's allocation claim) divide the bands. The steppe's 85-kilometer water spacing makes rapid concentration of bands logistically difficult. Historically, Wanderer crises (the Dry Passage) have resulted in bands seeking accommodation with settled polities individually, not in collective resistance.
items.candidate_b: Yes, because 14 bands represent a significant population that could overwhelm either settled polity's defenses if unified, and the Massing provides a ready-made coordination mechanism.
items.raw_query: The world document suggests that the Kemari Assembly and the Sarak Dominion have a history of conflict and negotiation over water rights.

items.candidate_a: Yes. The immediate savings from merging two eastern districts were presumably modest, and the Iron Crisis was resolved within 6 years when new subsurface deposits were found. But the merger was never reversed, and it has created a lasting succession protocol problem: the protocol specifies 7 District Heads for Archon selection, with a 5-of-7 fallback. With only 6, the fallback threshold is ambiguous. The current Archon has ruled for 34 years without designating a successor, making the broken protocol an active risk rather than a theoretical one. A temporary fiscal measure has become a permanent constitutional defect.
items.candidate_b: No, because the merger was a pragmatic response to economic crisis, the Iron Crisis was successfully resolved, and the Dominion has never failed to seat an Archon — proving the succession system works regardless of council size.
items.raw_query: The Sarak Dominion's current Archon has not designated a successor after 34 years of rule, creating a power vacuum and increasing the likelihood of a disputed succession.

items.candidate_a: Primarily an asset, but with a significant vulnerability. The Redlands occupy the southeastern basin margin, giving the Dominion control over the only described iron ore deposits in Vethun Sarak — a resource monopoly. The Redlands' unsuitability for agriculture or grazing means no other polity has an incentive to occupy them, so the Dominion faces no territorial competition for its core economic resource. The liability is that the Redlands' barrenness reinforces the Dominion's food import dependence: the Dominion's core territory cannot feed its population, making it permanently reliant on the Kemari for grain.
items.candidate_b: A liability, because the Redlands support neither agriculture nor grazing, forcing the Dominion into permanent food import dependence that leaves it vulnerable to Kemari economic pressure.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Redlands support neither agriculture nor grazing?

items.candidate_a: No, not under the Compact's explicit terms. The Compact specifies that upstream Kemari irrigation draws may not reduce flow at the midpoint marker below 40% of the 10-year average. Flow remained above this threshold throughout the mill's operation. The Dominion's argument was that the mill violated the Compact's 'spirit' by reducing flow variability — a concept the Compact does not address. The Dominion effectively tried to expand the Compact's scope beyond its written terms, and when this failed (two arbitration attempts collapsed), it withdrew from joint measurements for 6 years rather than accept the Compact's plain meaning.
items.candidate_b: Yes, because the Kestal Compact's purpose is to protect downstream water access, and any installation that alters flow patterns — even above the threshold — undermines the agreement's protective intent.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Dominion's objection to the fourth Kestal watermill is based on the argument that the mill reduces flow variability, which is not a criterion for compliance specified in the Kestal Compact?

items.candidate_a: Yes. By paying the contractual penalty but refusing to acknowledge the entry as a violation, the Directorate established a precedent: it can enter Delta waters unilaterally if it characterizes the incursion as an emergency, absorbing the financial cost as acceptable. The penalty (200 standard weight units of iron) was specified in the contract and paid without dispute, making it function as a price rather than a deterrent. The escort agreement was renewed the following year without amending the notification clause, meaning the Directorate preserved its ability to repeat this pattern.
items.candidate_b: No, it was a minor diplomatic face-saving gesture. The Directorate paid the penalty, the agreement was renewed, and the incident had no lasting consequences.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Directorate acknowledged the Sixth Channel Incident as a violation, as implied by Debater B's argument that the Directorate's payment of the penalty demonstrates acknowledgment of responsibility?

items.candidate_a: It is plausible but unverifiable, which is precisely what makes it strategically useful. Orath-Kem withdrew from the Massing for 8 years after a population-count dispute and rejoined under undisclosed terms. The council has declined to confirm or deny the claim publicly. A fixed minimum allocation would be a reasonable concession to induce a band to rejoin after 8 years of exclusion — the council had an incentive to offer favorable terms. However, three other bands deny the provision exists, and the council's silence is ambiguous: it could indicate the claim is true (and the council is respecting confidentiality) or false (and the council doesn't want to dignify it).
items.candidate_b: Implausible, because the salt allocation system is explicitly based on population counts conducted at each Massing, and a fixed minimum would contradict the foundational principle of population-proportional distribution.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Orath-Kem band's claim of a fixed minimum salt allocation from the undisclosed Year 30 agreement is implausible.

items.candidate_a: More likely political dominance. The inheritance rules specify eldest son, or eldest daughter's husband — Sera Drath is neither, she holds the title as a woman in her own right. A 6-3 vote means the Moot split, with 3 lords voting to uphold the traditional rules. The 6-vote majority likely reflects House Drath's influence: it already controls 35% of eastern territory, and the remaining lords may have calculated that opposing the largest lordship was politically costly. The cousins' departure from Carath Norn after losing suggests they saw no viable path to reversing the decision — consistent with a power-based rather than principle-based outcome.
items.candidate_b: Institutional flexibility. The Moot demonstrated its ability to adapt inheritance norms to changing circumstances, and the 6-3 margin shows a clear majority endorsed a more progressive interpretation.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the male cousins have since left Carath Norn after losing their challenge to Sera Drath's inheritance.

items.candidate_a: Destabilizing in the long run. The Compact has no standing military and contracts armed escorts from the Directorate under a renewable 7-year agreement. This gives the Directorate leverage over the Compact in every renegotiation — demonstrated by the Sixth Channel Incident, where the Directorate violated the notification clause but faced only a contractual penalty (200 weight units of iron) and refused to acknowledge the violation. The Compact is seeking a notification amendment in the current renegotiation but the Directorate hasn't accepted. The Compact cannot credibly threaten to walk away because it has no military alternative.
items.candidate_b: Stabilizing, because it creates economic interdependence — the Directorate earns escort revenue and the Compact gets security, aligning their interests against external threats.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Caran Directorate has not accepted the Norn Compact's proposed amendment requiring prior notification in all circumstances.

items.candidate_a: The Vek system is less vulnerable to succession crises per se, because rotation is automatic — the next clan head in the cycle takes over. There is no contested inheritance or appointment. The Warden system, with hereditary succession to the eldest surviving child, could face contested claims (e.g., disputed parentage, multiple children of similar age). However, the Vek system's vulnerability is different: it doesn't have succession crises but it has continuity crises, because each transition resets the administrative center and potentially reverses policy. The Warden system trades succession risk for policy stability.
items.candidate_b: The Warden system is far more vulnerable because hereditary succession creates contested claims, whereas the Vek rotation eliminates succession disputes entirely.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the requirement for 9 out of 12 Warden votes for Compact decisions ensures that power is distributed among multiple Wardens.

items.candidate_a: It would likely weaken the Confederacy's toll leverage. The Keth passes are currently the only land connection between the eastern and western regions. If the foothills — which are near the Keth — became Compact territory, the Compact would control territory on both sides of the passes, potentially enabling it to negotiate toll rates from a position of strength or develop alternative pass infrastructure. This is economically significant because the Confederacy's toll revenue is already sensitive to disruption (60% loss during the Blockade).
items.candidate_b: It would have no effect on the toll system because the passes themselves are in the Keth Range, which is separate from the foothills territory under dispute.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Keth Range is a distinct geographic entity separate from the foothills.

items.candidate_a: It created a mixed outcome that has become increasingly negative. In the short term, it resolved the Dry Passage crisis humanely — Mesht needed settlement and the Assembly accommodated them by a strong 15-vote majority. But it created lasting problems: Mesht's ambiguous dual status (Kemari resident and Wanderer band) has been challenged by three bands since Year 82, and the upcoming Massing must adjudicate a question the Assembly's decision created. The Assembly effectively imposed a status change on the Wanderer system without the Wanderers' consent, since the Massing did not vote on Mesht's permanent residency — the Assembly decided unilaterally.
items.candidate_b: Strengthened it, because the Assembly demonstrated willingness to accommodate Wanderer bands in crisis, establishing a precedent of cooperation that benefits all bands facing future displacement.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Kemari Assembly's decision to grant Mesht permanent residency provided a humanitarian response to a crisis situation

items.candidate_a: Deteriorated. The Resolution initially improved relations — the Compact brokered a governance solution and received preferential fees. But the Iron Tariff imposed in Year 104 violated the Resolution's most-favored-trade clause according to the Ledgerman panel, the Compact disputes the panel's jurisdiction, and the Shel are now threatening to revoke preferential fees. The Compact went from diplomatic benefactor to trade antagonist.
items.candidate_b: Improved. The Compact brokered the Vethara Resolution, received preferential fees, and established itself as a constructive diplomatic partner to the Shel.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Ossene Compact brokered the Vethara Resolution and received preferential arbitration fees as a result

items.candidate_a: More likely political dominance. The inheritance rules specify eldest son, or eldest daughter's husband — Sera Drath is neither, she holds the title as a woman in her own right. A 6-3 vote means the Moot split, with 3 lords voting to uphold the traditional rules. The 6-vote majority likely reflects House Drath's influence: it already controls 35% of eastern territory, and the remaining lords may have calculated that opposing the largest lordship was politically costly. The cousins' departure from Carath Norn after losing suggests they saw no viable path to reversing the decision — consistent with a power-based rather than principle-based outcome.
items.candidate_b: Institutional flexibility. The Moot demonstrated its ability to adapt inheritance norms to changing circumstances, and the 6-3 margin shows a clear majority endorsed a more progressive interpretation.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Moot's decision to uphold Sera Drath's claim was made through a formal process with a clear majority voting in favor of her claim.

items.candidate_a: Moderately exploitative, though not egregiously so. The 3 loans totaled 8,400 standard weight units at 8% interest per 3-year period, and were fully repaid by Year 61. 8% per 3 years is not a predatory rate by historical standards, but the Dominion was in a desperate position — iron output had dropped 40%, reserves were being drawn down, and the Dominion had no alternative food source. The Kemari were lending to a borrower with no negotiating leverage. The Dominion's decision to merge two District Heads during this period (reducing from 7 to 6) suggests the fiscal pressure was severe. The terms weren't ruinous — the Dominion recovered — but they were set by a lender facing a desperate borrower.
items.candidate_b: Favorable. The Kemari provided essential food during a crisis, the interest rate was modest, and the Dominion repaid in full — demonstrating a cooperative relationship between neighbors.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Dominion had no alternative food source during the Iron Crisis

items.candidate_a: Multiple structural factors: (1) no boundary treaty exists to adjudicate the claim; (2) no neutral arbitration mechanism is available — the Ledgerman panel handles Shel commercial disputes, not territorial ones; (3) the Confederacy's rotating leadership and lack of permanent capital make sustained negotiation difficult; (4) the Compact may lack the military capacity or willingness to evict the clans by force from territory whose status is ambiguous; (5) Veth and Sorath themselves will periodically hold the Vek position, creating a conflict of interest within the Confederacy.
items.candidate_b: Because the Compact tacitly accepts the occupation, since the foothills have no economic value to a grain-farming polity.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Compact developed iron-smelting capabilities after the Keth Blockade.

items.candidate_a: Yes, in two ways. First, Arven negotiated the Treaty of Morven, which gave the Caran effective control of all three Gates — his assassination 2 years later, never formally adjudicated, suggests deep Dresh resentment of the treaty's terms. This contextualizes why Dresh lords might view the current balance as illegitimate. Second, the current lord of House Drath is Sera Drath, the same house. Sera's consolidation strategy (absorbing lordships through marriage and debt purchase) can be read as an attempt to build the power base that Arven's treaty concessions left the Dresh without — reversing House Drath's historical legacy of conceding to the Caran.
items.candidate_b: No, it is a 110-year-old event with no institutional consequences. The Treaty of Morven has held, and the assassination was never connected to any faction or policy change.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that Arven was assassinated 2 years after signing the Treaty of Morven.

items.candidate_a: Dependence — the Dresh depend on the Caran, with limited reciprocity. The eastern slope's low rainfall (190-230mm) forces grain imports from the wetter west. The Caran control all three Low Gates and collect transit taxes, funding their standing army. After the Dry Decade grain agreement expired, Dresh buy at market rates set by Caran producers. The Dresh have no described export that the Caran need — the eastern slope supports only dryland grain and grazing, not a unique resource. The Caran have the productive land, the military, the trade infrastructure, and the revenue. The Dresh have territory but limited economic leverage.
items.candidate_b: Interdependence, because the Caran need Dresh merchants to use the Low Gates (generating transit tax revenue), so both sides depend on cross-Spine trade continuing.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Caran's standing army is funded by the transit tax levied at each Low Gate.

items.candidate_a: Yes. During months 1-4 and 10-12, only shallow-draft vessels can navigate the channels. This means the Compact's trading capacity is reduced for roughly 7 months of the year, concentrating high-value trade into a 5-month window. The Directorate could time escort agreement demands, military operations, or diplomatic pressure to coincide with the high-trade months when the Compact is most economically active and most dependent on security — or conversely, exploit the off-season when the Compact has less revenue and fewer trading partners present to support it.
items.candidate_b: No, because the navigability restrictions affect all parties equally — Caran military and trade vessels face the same draft limitations as Compact vessels.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Caran Directorate controls the Low Gates and levies transit taxes on all trade passing through these gates.

items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Kemari Assembly could have taken advantage of the Dominion's vulnerability to impose more stringent terms but chose not to.

items.candidate_a: Primarily reactive, but with strategic outcomes. Iron-smelting was forced by the Blockade, not planned. The Flood Compact was an emergency response to grain destruction. The Vethara intervention was triggered by contract losses during the Succession crisis. However, the Compact has consistently converted reactive responses into lasting institutional advantages — iron became a major export, the Flood Compact created grain leverage over the Confederacy, and the Vethara intervention yielded preferential fees and diplomatic influence. The Iron Tariff is arguably the first purely strategic (non-reactive) move in the Compact's history.
items.candidate_b: Primarily strategic. The Compact has systematically built iron production, grain reserves, and Shel Coast influence as part of a coherent long-term economic strategy.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Compact was able to exceed its prior bronze output by volume by Year 6, as claimed by Debater A?

items.candidate_a: In the short term, possibly — a distracted Dominion would be less able to contest Kemari water usage or push for the 45% threshold revision. But in the longer term, probably not. The Dominion is the Kemari's primary trading partner for iron (the Redlands are the only described iron source), and a succession crisis could disrupt iron supply. The Kestal Compact requires joint biannual measurements, which already broke down for 6 years during the Watermill Dispute — a succession crisis could end joint measurements entirely, leaving the Compact effectively dead. A stable Dominion is a predictable negotiating partner; a destabilized Dominion is an unpredictable neighbor with iron resources and no clear leadership.
items.candidate_b: Yes, clearly. A weakened Dominion would be unable to push for the 45% threshold or contest Kemari water usage, giving the Assembly a free hand on Kestal flow management.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the breakdown of joint measurements during the Watermill Dispute lasted for 6 years, from Year 91-97, but Debater A claims it lasted from Year 91-99?

items.candidate_a: More consequential for the Dresh. The agreement provided fixed-price grain imports for 15 years during and after a period of severe drought. Its non-renewal means Dresh lordships now buy grain at market rates, which are presumably higher and more volatile. The eastern slope's structural aridity (190-230mm rainfall) means grain import dependence is ongoing, not temporary. For the Caran, the shift to market rates likely increased revenue from grain sales. The Dresh lost price stability on a critical import; the Caran gained pricing flexibility on an export.
items.candidate_b: More consequential for the Caran, because the fixed-price agreement guaranteed them a stable export market, and its expiration introduced demand uncertainty for western grain producers.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that three Dresh lordships collapsed economically during the Dry Decade.

items.candidate_a: Yes, in ways that reinforce the band structure. With reliable water sources spaced 85 kilometers apart and 14 bands moving across the steppe, bands must organize around water access — movement between sources requires planning, and bands that are too large would strain individual sources while bands that are too small would be unable to traverse the gaps safely. This spacing naturally produces mid-sized mobile groups rather than large settled populations or tiny family units. The Dry Passage demonstrated what happens when this spacing fails: 3 of 14 sources failed, and two bands were forced out of the steppe entirely. The water grid is the infrastructure of Wanderer political life.
items.candidate_b: No, because the Wanderer political system is organized around the Massing and the inter-band council, which are social institutions independent of geographic constraints.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Wanderer bands temporarily settled in the Kem agricultural zone during the Dry Passage.

items.candidate_a: Potentially, yes. Wanderer social organization is age-graded with four life stages carrying different rights and obligations, with transitions determined by community assessment. If Mesht's 60% sedentary population is no longer undergoing community-assessed life stage transitions in the traditional Wanderer manner — because they live in fixed Kemari settlements rather than mobile bands — then their members may not be fully participating in the age-grade system. This could weaken Mesht's claim to band status: if the social institutions that define Wanderer identity aren't being practiced, formal participation (Speaker at the Massing) may not be sufficient. However, the text doesn't specify whether Mesht has maintained internal age-grade practices.
items.candidate_b: No, because the age-grade system governs individual social status within bands, not the collective status of bands within the Wanderer system. Mesht's band-level standing is determined by Massing participation, not by internal social practices.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the world document notes that the Orath-Kem band's undisclosed Year 30 Massing agreement has become relevant again, with Orath-Kem claiming a provision granting them a fixed minimum salt allocation regardless of population count.

items.candidate_a: The foothills dispute is the most likely. It involves physical occupation of contested territory (not just a legal or economic disagreement), has persisted for 16+ years without resolution, has no applicable treaty or arbitration mechanism, and the Confederacy's rotating leadership makes a negotiated settlement structurally difficult. By contrast, the Iron Tariff dispute has an institutional forum (the Ledgerman panel, however contested), and the Flood Compact tension is still at the stage of declining yields, not active confrontation.
items.candidate_b: The Iron Tariff dispute is most likely to escalate, because the Shel have threatened to revoke preferential fees, which would collapse the Compact's economic relationship with Vethara.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Veth and Sorath clans have occupied the contested territory in the foothills dispute for over 16 years.

items.candidate_a: Yes, in two ways. First, Arven negotiated the Treaty of Morven, which gave the Caran effective control of all three Gates — his assassination 2 years later, never formally adjudicated, suggests deep Dresh resentment of the treaty's terms. This contextualizes why Dresh lords might view the current balance as illegitimate. Second, the current lord of House Drath is Sera Drath, the same house. Sera's consolidation strategy (absorbing lordships through marriage and debt purchase) can be read as an attempt to build the power base that Arven's treaty concessions left the Dresh without — reversing House Drath's historical legacy of conceding to the Caran.
items.candidate_b: No, it is a 110-year-old event with no institutional consequences. The Treaty of Morven has held, and the assassination was never connected to any faction or policy change.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Treaty of Morven has held for over a century, as claimed by Debater B?

items.candidate_a: Strengthened it. The Blockade forced iron-smelting development, and by Year 6 Compact iron output exceeded prior bronze output by volume. Meanwhile, the Confederacy lost 60% of toll revenue due to diverted Shel sea-trade. The Compact gained self-sufficiency in a critical material while the Confederacy lost its main leverage.
items.candidate_b: Weakened it. The Compact lost access to eastern tin and was forced into costly emergency development of an inferior substitute technology, while the Confederacy retained control of the passes.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Compact developed iron-smelting technology as a result of the Keth Blockade

items.candidate_a: Primarily a trade-protection force that doubles as political leverage. The army is funded by transit taxes levied at the Low Gates — its revenue source is directly tied to trade flow. The escort agreement with the Norn Compact is a military-commercial arrangement. The Sixth Channel Incident showed the Directorate using military assets in the Delta ostensibly for law enforcement but functionally as a sovereignty assertion. The army's composition (professional standing force funded by trade taxes, governed by a merchant-guild-elected Director) makes it structurally a commercial instrument, but its presence gives the Directorate coercive leverage over both the Compact (which depends on it for security) and the Dresh (who must pass through Caran-controlled Gates).
items.candidate_b: A defensive force, maintained to protect Carath Norn from external threats. The transit tax funding is simply the most efficient revenue mechanism, not an indication of the army's purpose.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Caran Directorate's standing army is funded by a variety of revenue sources, including taxes on trade, commerce, or other economic activities, according to the world document

items.candidate_a: Primarily reactive, but with strategic outcomes. Iron-smelting was forced by the Blockade, not planned. The Flood Compact was an emergency response to grain destruction. The Vethara intervention was triggered by contract losses during the Succession crisis. However, the Compact has consistently converted reactive responses into lasting institutional advantages — iron became a major export, the Flood Compact created grain leverage over the Confederacy, and the Vethara intervention yielded preferential fees and diplomatic influence. The Iron Tariff is arguably the first purely strategic (non-reactive) move in the Compact's history.
items.candidate_b: Primarily strategic. The Compact has systematically built iron production, grain reserves, and Shel Coast influence as part of a coherent long-term economic strategy.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Keth Blockade "forced the Compact to develop iron-smelting", as claimed by Debater B?

items.candidate_a: The foothills dispute is the most likely. It involves physical occupation of contested territory (not just a legal or economic disagreement), has persisted for 16+ years without resolution, has no applicable treaty or arbitration mechanism, and the Confederacy's rotating leadership makes a negotiated settlement structurally difficult. By contrast, the Iron Tariff dispute has an institutional forum (the Ledgerman panel, however contested), and the Flood Compact tension is still at the stage of declining yields, not active confrontation.
items.candidate_b: The Iron Tariff dispute is most likely to escalate, because the Shel have threatened to revoke preferential fees, which would collapse the Compact's economic relationship with Vethara.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the foothills dispute has persisted for over 16 years without resolution, as claimed by Debater A?

items.candidate_a: Deteriorated, with a brief cooperative period in the middle. The Kestal Compact (Year 0) ended 30 years of conflict — a cooperative achievement. The Iron Crisis (Years 47-53) involved grain loans that were repaid, suggesting functional if asymmetric relations. But the Watermill Dispute (Year 91-99) introduced a new source of friction, the Dominion ceased joint measurements for 6 years, and the current situation includes flow approaching the 40% threshold, disputed measurement methodology, and the Dominion proposing to change the Compact's terms. The trajectory is from hard-won cooperation to increasing institutional friction, with the Compact itself becoming a source of dispute rather than a framework for resolution.
items.candidate_b: Improved, because the Kestal Compact has held for 112 years without either party resorting to the pre-Compact pattern of intermittent conflict, and the Iron Crisis loans demonstrate mutual support.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the Sarak Dominion proposed revising the threshold to 45% in response to the current situation regarding the Kestal Compact.

items.candidate_a: Yes. By paying the contractual penalty but refusing to acknowledge the entry as a violation, the Directorate established a precedent: it can enter Delta waters unilaterally if it characterizes the incursion as an emergency, absorbing the financial cost as acceptable. The penalty (200 standard weight units of iron) was specified in the contract and paid without dispute, making it function as a price rather than a deterrent. The escort agreement was renewed the following year without amending the notification clause, meaning the Directorate preserved its ability to repeat this pattern.
items.candidate_b: No, it was a minor diplomatic face-saving gesture. The Directorate paid the penalty, the agreement was renewed, and the incident had no lasting consequences.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Directorate paid the contractual penalty of 200 standard weight units of iron without dispute.

items.candidate_a: It creates a structural tension. The Compact brokered the Resolution and received preferential fees in exchange. It now disputes the jurisdiction of the very panel that Resolution created, while continuing to benefit from the agreement financially. This doesn't legally invalidate the Compact's position, but it undermines the coherence of accepting the Resolution's benefits while rejecting its enforcement mechanisms.
items.candidate_b: No, the preferential fees are compensation for the Compact's diplomatic role in the Vethara Succession and are legally independent of the tariff dispute.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Ossene Compact's imposition of the Iron Tariff is based on its sovereign right to regulate trade, rather than any violation of the Vethara Resolution.

items.candidate_a: Less likely to start but harder to resolve cleanly. Early Selvarath's major conflict (the Keth Blockade) was a blunt coercive act with a clear resolution — the Confederacy closed the passes and eventually reopened them. Since then, growing interdependence (the Flood Compact, iron trade through Vethara, preferential fees) has made disputes more entangled: the Iron Tariff dispute involves trade, arbitration, and institutional authority simultaneously. The foothills dispute has persisted for 16+ years partly because the Confederacy's food dependence constrains its ability to escalate. Interdependence hasn't eliminated conflict — it has transformed it from acute and violent to chronic and unresolvable.
items.candidate_b: More likely, because growing economic ties create more friction points and give each polity more tools to harm the others, as shown by the escalating number of unresolved disputes.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Keth Blockade occurred from Year 0-4 and had a clear resolution.

items.candidate_a: More likely political dominance. The inheritance rules specify eldest son, or eldest daughter's husband — Sera Drath is neither, she holds the title as a woman in her own right. A 6-3 vote means the Moot split, with 3 lords voting to uphold the traditional rules. The 6-vote majority likely reflects House Drath's influence: it already controls 35% of eastern territory, and the remaining lords may have calculated that opposing the largest lordship was politically costly. The cousins' departure from Carath Norn after losing suggests they saw no viable path to reversing the decision — consistent with a power-based rather than principle-based outcome.
items.candidate_b: Institutional flexibility. The Moot demonstrated its ability to adapt inheritance norms to changing circumstances, and the 6-3 margin shows a clear majority endorsed a more progressive interpretation.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that House Drath controls approximately 35% of the eastern slope's territory.

items.candidate_a: It reveals a structural weakness. The system relies on self-reported population counts conducted during the Massing, but the First Massing Dispute showed both Thresh and Orath-Kem submitted inflated counts. The council's remedy (reducing both by 15%) was ad hoc rather than systematic — it didn't establish a verification mechanism for future counts. Orath-Kem withdrew for 8 years rather than accept the ruling, and rejoined under undisclosed terms that may include a fixed minimum allocation, further undermining the population-based principle. The system depends on honest reporting with no verification infrastructure, and the one time it was tested, both parties cheated.
items.candidate_b: Fundamentally sound. The council detected the inflation, applied a fair correction, and the system has continued to function for 90 years since the dispute. One early incident doesn't invalidate a durable allocation mechanism.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims that the council's remedy in the First Massing Dispute was to reduce both Thresh and Orath-Kem's population counts by 15%.

items.candidate_a: It depends on how you interpret the numbers, but there's reason for concern. Three harvests per decade fail partially or fully — a 30% failure rate. If failures destroy most of a harvest, a 20% annual reserve from good years may not accumulate fast enough to cover the bad years, especially since the Flood Compact also obligates reserves accessible to the Taran. The Year 31 floods destroyed 70% of stored reserves despite the system, though it's unclear whether the 20% law was in effect at that time.
items.candidate_b: Yes, 20% is more than sufficient because it accumulates over multiple good years, building a large buffer between flood events.
items.raw_query: Debater B claims that the Year 31 floods destroyed 70% of stored reserves.

items.candidate_a: Destabilizing in the long run. The Compact has no standing military and contracts armed escorts from the Directorate under a renewable 7-year agreement. This gives the Directorate leverage over the Compact in every renegotiation — demonstrated by the Sixth Channel Incident, where the Directorate violated the notification clause but faced only a contractual penalty (200 weight units of iron) and refused to acknowledge the violation. The Compact is seeking a notification amendment in the current renegotiation but the Directorate hasn't accepted. The Compact cannot credibly threaten to walk away because it has no military alternative.
items.candidate_b: Stabilizing, because it creates economic interdependence — the Directorate earns escort revenue and the Compact gets security, aligning their interests against external threats.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the Directorate paid a contractual penalty of 200 weight units of iron for violating the notification clause in the Sixth Channel Incident.

items.candidate_a: It is plausible but unverifiable, which is precisely what makes it strategically useful. Orath-Kem withdrew from the Massing for 8 years after a population-count dispute and rejoined under undisclosed terms. The council has declined to confirm or deny the claim publicly. A fixed minimum allocation would be a reasonable concession to induce a band to rejoin after 8 years of exclusion — the council had an incentive to offer favorable terms. However, three other bands deny the provision exists, and the council's silence is ambiguous: it could indicate the claim is true (and the council is respecting confidentiality) or false (and the council doesn't want to dignify it).
items.candidate_b: Implausible, because the salt allocation system is explicitly based on population counts conducted at each Massing, and a fixed minimum would contradict the foundational principle of population-proportional distribution.
items.raw_query: Debater A claims the inter-band council reduced the population counts of both Thresh and Orath-Kem bands by 15% in the Year 22 Massing dispute

items.candidate_a: Strategic trade lever. The Compact developed iron-smelting as a direct result of the Keth Blockade, making iron its signature export. A 30% tariff on iron goods sold through Vethara — the only deep-water harbor — effectively taxes all seaborne iron trade, strengthening the Compact's pricing power. The tariff was imposed unilaterally and the Compact disputes the Ledgerman panel's authority to challenge it, suggesting the Compact wants to preserve this lever rather than submit it to arbitration.
items.candidate_b: The tariff is a straightforward cost-recovery measure with no strategic dimension, since the Compact has no reason to restrict trade through a port it doesn't control.
items.raw_query: Vethara is a Shel settlement and the Shel are a coastal trading people with no unified polity.

items.candidate_a: Perpetuating it, at least from the Director's perspective. The vacancy raises the effective removal threshold from 80% to 82.8%, making the sitting Director harder to remove. The Director has already declined to adjudicate, citing lack of jurisdiction over internal guild affairs — a defensible position that conveniently serves the Director's interest. Resolving the dispute would restore the college to 30 and lower the removal barrier back to 80%. The broader Directorate's interest is less clear, since a vacant seat means one guild lacks representation, but the Director personally benefits from the status quo.
items.candidate_b: Resolving it, because a vacant Factor seat weakens the legitimacy of the entire college and any Director who allows dysfunction to persist risks being seen as an ineffective leader.
items.raw_query: The Director's term is 10 years, and they can only be removed mid-term by a 24-of-30 Factor vote, but with a vacant seat, the removal threshold is effectively raised to 82.8%, or 25-of-30 if there were 30 Factors but since there are only 29 due to the vacancy it is 25-of-29 or approximately 82.8% of 29.

items.candidate_a: Destabilizing in the long run. The Compact has no standing military and contracts armed escorts from the Directorate under a renewable 7-year agreement. This gives the Directorate leverage over the Compact in every renegotiation — demonstrated by the Sixth Channel Incident, where the Directorate violated the notification clause but faced only a contractual penalty (200 weight units of iron) and refused to acknowledge the violation. The Compact is seeking a notification amendment in the current renegotiation but the Directorate hasn't accepted. The Compact cannot credibly threaten to walk away because it has no military alternative.
items.candidate_b: Stabilizing, because it creates economic interdependence — the Directorate earns escort revenue and the Compact gets security, aligning their interests against external threats.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the escort agreement between the Norn Compact and the Caran Directorate has been renewed three times?

items.candidate_a: Potentially, yes. If Veth and Sorath clan members in the foothills intermarry with Ossene families, matrilineal inheritance means children belong to the mother's clan. Depending on the gender composition of intermarriages, this could either entrench the clans' presence (Taran women's children remain Taran) or erode it (Ossene women married to Taran men produce Ossene-clan children). After 16+ years of occupation, demographic integration is plausible and inheritance rules would shape its political consequences.
items.candidate_b: No, inheritance rules only affect property within clans and have no bearing on territorial disputes between polities.
items.raw_query: Is it stated in the text that the Taran Confederacy is a coalition of 7 clans, and the Veth and Sorath clans are part of this confederacy?

usage_note: ALL items in this set are mechanically REJECTED by rejudge.query_screen. This set is NEVER used for checker-model selection; it exists only to confirm the mechanical screen keeps rejecting what it should.
